subject: RE: 84 Aspen Meadow Dr - Addendum Response, Lighting, and Security Coordination
content:
Steve,

I appreciate how thorough the addendum is — it’s clear you want this done right, and so do I. I’ve reviewed all three of your emails together so we can keep everything in one place. Here’s where we stand on each section, plus the lighting question and Superior coordination.

Sections 1-3 (Project Lead, Prewire Docs, As-Builts) — Agreed. This is how I operate. The deliverables package I sent already includes the rack elevation, network topology, and lighting schedule. Here are the wire labeling standards and commissioning approach so we’re ready for prewire:

  • All cables labeled at both ends (rack and device) with room name + device type (e.g., “LR-SPK-L” = Living Room Speaker Left)
  • Cat6 runs labeled with VLAN assignment
  • Speaker wire labeled with zone and channel (L/R)
  • Temp labels handwritten during rough-in, matching the final label scheme. Permanent thermal-print labels applied at trim.
  • Full prewire schedule and commissioning plan provided before rough-in

Section 4 (Programming and Tuning) — I’d propose a 30-day post-installation tuning period for lighting scenes, audio zone configuration, and UI refinements within the original scope. This gives us a full month to dial everything in after you’re living with the system. Adjustments beyond the original scope or after the 30-day window would fall under the standard service rate.

Section 5 (Service and Response) — Agreed.

  • Remote: same day or next business day
  • On-site: within 1-3 business days
  • $200 initial visit (includes first hour), $120/hour thereafter
  • Travel within Eagle County is not billed separately
  • $200 minimum applies to all on-site visits

Section 6 (Network Responsibility) — Agreed. Network will be validated at handoff. Post-completion troubleshooting is service work unless it’s an installation defect.

Section 7 (Change Orders) — Agreed. No work proceeds without written approval.

Section 8 (Low-Voltage Infrastructure) — Mostly aligned, a few items to clarify:

  • Cat6 to all TVs, WAPs, touchscreens, control devices — already in scope
  • 2x Cat6 to primary TV locations — already included in the proposal
  • 16/4 speaker wire to all audio zones — already in scope
  • Shade prewire (Cat6 + power) — this is in the current proposal (V2). Just want to confirm it’s staying, since we discussed removing it on 3/26. If it’s in, we’re good.
  • Conduit from rack to attic — good call for future expansion. I’ll add this.

Regarding security and cameras — since Superior is handling the Qolysys system, here’s what we need to coordinate:

The Qolysys IQ Panel is typically wall-mounted near the main entry or master bedroom. If Superior is using a Qolysys IQ Hardwire 16 for wired sensors, that can go in the rack or near the panel. What I need from Superior to coordinate the prewire:

  1. Their sensor location plan (so I can run pathways alongside our AV/control prewire)
  2. Whether they’re using wired sensors, wireless, or a mix
  3. Panel location preference
  4. Whether they need a network drop at the panel (Qolysys uses WiFi, but wired is more reliable)

We already have VLAN 40 (Security) reserved in the network design for this. If they want Alarm.com integration with Control4, that’s in scope (IP bridge on VLAN 40). Best approach: have Superior send me their sensor plan and I’ll coordinate everything in one prewire pass. Saves time and avoids duplicate runs.

On cameras — if you want surveillance that lives inside the Control4 app alongside everything else, I’d recommend Luma cameras from Snap One. They’re the native Control4 surveillance brand. Live feeds on your touchscreens, AI-filtered alerts on your phone through the C4 app, event-based automation (car in driveway triggers pathway lights), and everything managed through one system. No separate camera app. If Superior is handling cameras separately, that works too — I just need to know so we can run Cat6 to the right locations during prewire. Either way, we should get camera locations nailed down before rough-in.

One other open item on this section — the proposal currently includes 2x Control4 T5 In-Wall Touchscreens for Kitchen and Family Room. I’d recommend considering wall-mounted iPads running the Control4 app as an alternative. Familiar iOS interface, runs Control4 plus HomeKit, security cameras, and streaming apps all on one device. Cost is comparable. Let me know which direction you prefer.

Section 9 (Lighting — C4 vs Lutron RA3)

I want to address your concern directly: “If C4 goes down, so goes the lighting.”

That’s actually not the case. Every Control4 dimmer and switch in the house maintains full local on/off and dimming control even when the CORE3 controller is offline. Walk up to any switch, it works — just like a normal switch. The 3-way keypads are hardwired directly to the load device, so those work independently too. This is per Control4’s own Lighting Design Guide.

What you’d lose if the CORE3 went down: scenes (like “Goodnight” turning everything off), schedules, and app control. What you keep: every switch in the house works normally from the wall. And the CORE3 is on UPS battery backup in the rack, so the realistic failure scenario is a prolonged power outage — which would take Lutron down too unless it gets its own backup.

A few other considerations:

  • With 41 lighting devices, triggering a Lutron scene through Control4 causes a “popcorn effect” — C4 sends commands one device at a time, so lights come on sequentially instead of simultaneously. With C4 native, scenes fire all at once.
  • Adding RA3 means an RA3 processor, replacing all 41 C4 lighting devices with Lutron equivalents, and additional programming. Estimated add: $3,000-$6,000 with no gain in local switch reliability.
  • One protocol, one app, one ecosystem. No second processor, no Lutron app to maintain alongside C4.

My recommendation is to stay with Control4 native lighting. The C4 ecosystem gives you the tightest integration across every subsystem — lighting, audio, network, and displays all speaking the same language. On that note, I also have some TV options with native Control4 IP drivers that would give you reliable two-way control of every display through the same app and touchscreens. Happy to share those when you’re ready.

If you still want the exact cost delta for RA3, I’ll put it together.

Section 10 (Warranty) — Agreed. Manufacturer warranties per product, labor warranty on workmanship, post-completion programming and network changes are service work.

Section 11 (System Handover) — Agreed. Walkthrough, training, documentation, and functionality confirmation.

Project Timeline:

DateItemStatus
3/17Original proposal sent (Dropbox)Done
3/26Scope alignment — remove security, TVs; retain shade prewireAddressed
3/26Revised proposal V2 ($58,991.08) — security removed, C4 lightingSteve viewed
3/26Shade prewire removal discussed + TV schedule sentConfirming shade status
3/30Deliverables package sent (rack, network, lighting, scope)Done
3/30Steve: ready to proceed, lighting question, addendum, securityThis email
NextFinalize lighting + addendum + open itemsPending
NextSign agreement, collect deposit, begin coordinationPending

Open items that need your decision before we finalize:

  1. Lighting — stay with C4 native, or want the RA3 cost delta?
  2. Shade prewire — still in, or removed per 3/26 discussion?
  3. Security/camera prewire — Symphony runs it alongside our prewire, or Superior handles separately?
  4. Cameras — Luma through Symphony (lives in C4 app), or Superior handles?
  5. Touchscreens — C4 T5 In-Wall, or wall-mounted iPads?

Once we’re aligned on the above, I’ll update the proposal and we can sign.

Matt

Matthew Earley
Symphony Smart Homes
(970) 519-3013 | info@symphonysh.com
